title: What Is a Phase II Archaeological Evaluation?
content:
When a Phase I archaeological survey identifies a potentially significant site, the next critical step is a Phase II archaeological evaluation. This targeted investigation answers one pivotal question: does the site qualify for listing on the National Register of Historic Places (NRHP)? The answer determines whether a federal undertaking can proceed as planned or must be altered to protect cultural resources.

Quick Answer: A Phase II archaeological evaluation is a targeted excavation and analysis study that determines whether an archaeological site meets the National Register of Historic Places eligibility criteria. It involves systematic test units, artifact analysis, and a technical report used by federal agencies and State Historic Preservation Offices to make formal eligibility determinations.

For federal agencies, state departments of transportation, and private developers working on federally permitted or funded projects, Phase II evaluations are a central component of Section 106 compliance under the National Historic Preservation Act (NHPA). Understanding what this process involves—and what it costs in time and resources—helps project teams plan effectively and avoid regulatory surprises.

Why Is a Phase II Evaluation Required?

A Phase II evaluation is required when a Phase I survey identifies an archaeological site that cannot be dismissed as insignificant without further study. Federal regulations under 36 CFR Part 800 mandate that agencies determine the eligibility of any site that may be affected by a federal undertaking before approving it.

The National Register eligibility criteria (Criteria A through D) assess a site's association with important historical events or people, its architectural or artistic distinction, and its potential to yield significant information through research. Most archaeological sites are evaluated under Criterion D—information potential—because they can tell us things about the past that no other record can.

Without a Phase II evaluation, neither the lead federal agency nor the State Historic Preservation Office (SHPO) has sufficient data to make a defensible eligibility determination. Skipping this step exposes project proponents to legal challenge, project delays, and costly redesigns discovered late in construction.

What Methods Are Used in a Phase II Archaeological Evaluation?

Phase II investigations employ systematic excavation methods designed to characterize the site's depth, spatial extent, internal organization, and artifact density. The goal is to collect enough data to evaluate significance without fully excavating the site.

The primary method is the excavation of hand-dug test units—typically 1×1 meter or 1×2 meter squares placed across the site in a statistically guided pattern. Soil is screened through wire mesh to recover artifacts, faunal remains, botanical material, and other cultural debris. Feature identification—hearths, postholes, refuse pits—is a key objective because intact features often signal high site integrity and research value.

Additional techniques may include ground-penetrating radar (GPR) or magnetometry to map subsurface anomalies before excavating, as well as soil core sampling to assess stratigraphic integrity across the site area. All recovered materials are cataloged, analyzed in a laboratory, and interpreted in light of regional archaeological patterns and research questions.

How Is National Register Eligibility Determined?

Eligibility determination is a two-part judgment: the site must possess significance under at least one NRHP criterion, and it must retain integrity—the physical characteristics that convey that significance. A site can be historically important but still ineligible if disturbance has destroyed the evidence that makes it meaningful.

The Phase II report presents the archaeologist's professional recommendation regarding eligibility, supported by quantitative and qualitative analysis of the recovered data. This recommendation goes to the lead federal agency and the SHPO, who make a formal, concurrence-based determination. If both parties agree the site is not eligible, the project may proceed without further archaeological work in that area.

If the site is determined eligible, the project moves into Section 106 consultation to resolve adverse effects—either through project redesign to avoid the site or a Phase III data recovery excavation to document its values before impacts occur. The Phase II finding thus sits at the center of every subsequent cultural resource management decision.

How Long Does a Phase II Evaluation Take?

Timeline varies considerably based on site size, depth of deposits, laboratory processing needs, and the SHPO review schedule. For a moderately sized site, fieldwork typically runs one to three weeks, while laboratory analysis and report preparation add another four to ten weeks.

SHPO review after report submission can range from thirty days to several months, depending on the office's workload and whether they request additional information. Project teams should budget a minimum of three to six months from the start of fieldwork to a final eligibility determination for most projects.

Factors that lengthen the process include encountering deeply buried deposits requiring heavy equipment, large artifact assemblages requiring extensive analysis, disagreements between the agency and SHPO requiring additional consultation, and the involvement of Tribes under the Native American Graves Protection and Repatriation Act (NAGPRA) or Section 106 tribal consultation requirements. Early coordination with all consulting parties shortens timelines significantly.

What Does a Phase II Report Include?

The Phase II technical report is the primary deliverable and the evidentiary record for the eligibility determination. It must meet standards set by the Secretary of the Interior's Standards and Guidelines for Archaeology and Historic Preservation and any applicable state guidelines.

A complete Phase II report documents the project background and regulatory context, prior archaeological research in the study area, field methods and unit locations, stratigraphic profiles and feature descriptions, artifact inventories and analysis, faunal and botanical findings where applicable, radiocarbon or other dating results, and the archaeologist's eligibility recommendation with supporting rationale.

Maps, photographs, and tabular data are essential components. The report is submitted to the SHPO and federal agency, becomes part of the permanent administrative record, and is typically filed with the state site inventory. Confidentiality provisions protect precise site location data from public disclosure to prevent looting.

Frequently Asked Questions

What triggers the need for a Phase II archaeological evaluation?

A Phase II evaluation is triggered when a Phase I survey identifies an archaeological site within a project's area of potential effects that cannot be determined insignificant based on surface findings alone. Federal agencies and SHPOs require additional subsurface data before making a formal National Register eligibility determination.

Can a project proceed if a site is found eligible after Phase II?

Yes, but only after completing Section 106 consultation to resolve adverse effects. Options include redesigning the project to avoid the site entirely, implementing monitoring measures, or conducting a Phase III data recovery excavation to document the site's values before impacts. The lead federal agency has final authority after consulting with the SHPO and other parties.

Who is qualified to conduct a Phase II archaeological evaluation?

Phase II work must be directed by a Principal Investigator who meets the Secretary of the Interior's Professional Qualification Standards—typically a master's or doctoral degree in archaeology plus at least one year of professional experience. Many states also require project archaeologists to hold a state-issued permit or meet additional registration requirements.

How much does a Phase II archaeological evaluation cost?

Costs range widely—from roughly $15,000 for a small, shallow site to well over $100,000 for large or deeply stratified sites. The major cost drivers are the number of test units required, laboratory processing volume, and report preparation time. Obtaining competitive bids from qualified CRM firms and scoping the work carefully with the SHPO upfront keeps costs predictable.

Is Phase II work always required after Phase I?

No. Phase II is only required when Phase I identifies sites that need eligibility testing. If Phase I finds no sites, or finds only sites that can be clearly determined not eligible based on existing evidence, the project can proceed to the next stage without additional archaeological investigation.

Plan Your Phase II Evaluation with Expert Support

Phase II archaeological evaluations require precise field methods, rigorous laboratory analysis, and well-supported technical reporting to withstand agency and SHPO scrutiny. Cutting corners at this stage can undermine the entire compliance process and expose projects to avoidable delays and costs.

Tennessee Valley Archaeological Research (TVAR) brings RPA-certified archaeologists, more than 14 years of Southeast US project experience, and in-house GIS and remote sensing capabilities to every Phase II engagement. Our team understands the regulatory expectations of federal agencies and state SHPOs across the region and structures each investigation to produce defensible, efficient results.

Whether you are a federal agency, a state DOT, or a private developer navigating Section 106 compliance, TVAR can guide your project from Phase I identification through Phase II evaluation and beyond. Contact TVAR today to discuss your project's needs and timeline.
excerpt: A Phase II archaeological evaluation determines if a site is eligible for the National Register of Historic Places, guiding federal compliance and CRM decisions.
